summary: High‑energy set with killer Shakedown Street and soaring Morning Dew
blurb: The show bursts open with a tight Shakedown Street and rolls through strong picks like Deal, Candyman and Little Red Rooster. The second set shines on the Help > Slipknot > Franklin's Tower sequence and builds to an emotional Morning Dew finale. Jerry’s lead work is fiery throughout while Brent’s piano adds bright texture.
review: June 24, 1983 at the Dane County Coliseum captured one of the Dead’s most electrifying midsummer nights. The first set kicks off with a crisp, driving Shakedown Street that immediately puts the band in a high‑octane groove, and it never lets up – Candyman, Little Red Rooster (home to what many call Bob Weir’s best slide solo), Brown Eyed Women and Deal all land with punchy precision. The second set is where the night truly soars: the Help On The Way → Slipknot! → Franklin's Tower segue rolls seamlessly into a luminous Lost Sailor and a soaring Saint Of Circumstance, leading into a theatrical drum‑mask showdown before Space erupts into a marathon Truckin' that segues straight into an unforgettable Morning Dew. Jerry Garcia is on fire, delivering explosive leads on Deal and Saint and a heartfelt, goose‑bumps‑inducing climax on Dew, while Brent Mydland’s bright piano licks thread through the mix without eclipsing the guitars. Phil Lesh anchors the band with booming “bombs” on Shakedown Street and a crushing low end throughout, and Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann keep the rhythmic engine both tight and adventurous. For anyone hunting a quintessential early‑80s Dead experience – especially those who love a bright second‑set mix and an emotional Dew – this show is a must‑listen.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All seven recording analyses are positive, repeatedly praising the high‑energy performance, standout songs such as Shakedown Street, Deal, Help > Slipknot > Franklin's Tower and Morning Dew, and describing Jerry’s playing as explosive and the band as locked in. No negative comments were noted across any source.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard capture with excellent fidelity, bright second‑set mix and full set coverage that preserves every highlighted moment.

key_highlights: Shakedown Street opens the night with crisp, tight groove.
Help On The Way → Slipknot! → Franklin's Tower sequence rolls seamlessly into a soaring jam.
Morning Dew delivers an emotional climax with Jerry’s heartfelt lead.
Little Red Rooster features Bob Weir’s arguably best slide solo.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia opens with a huge smile, flawless tone on Shakedown and an explosive solo on Deal; his lead on Saint of Circumstance is “off the hook,” and he delivers an emotional, goose‑bumps‑inducing climax on Morning Dew, working the scales at the top of his game.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh smiles constantly, drops “bombs” throughout, delivers a memorable St. of Circumstance solo, crushes the low end on Shakedown Street and provides a blasting start that fuels the band’s fun vibe.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s slide work on Little Red Rooster is hailed as his best ever, adding a gritty bluesy edge to the set.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s simple piano shines with bright licks, providing great texture throughout; occasionally overshadows Jerry during Dew but pulls back later, and a “Brent number” spotlights his keyboard flair.
band_performance.Mickey: Together with Billy, Mickey adds theatrical flair – bat‑mask drum solo, talking‑drum textures and powerful playing that drive the second set’s intensity.
band_performance.Billy: Pairs with Mickey for dynamic percussion, delivering a mighty‑fine drum segment after the Bobby jam and keeping the rhythmic engine tight and adventurous.
